seoTitle: QinHeng Products | USB Converters, MCUs & Wireless | LiTong
seoDescription: QinHeng product catalog featuring USB-to-serial converters, RISC-V microcontrollers, BLE SoCs, and interface bridges for embedded applications.

faqs.question: What product categories does QinHeng offer?
faqs.answer: QinHeng offers four main product categories: USB-to-Serial Converters including CH340 and CH341 for UART, SPI, I2C interfaces; USB Microcontrollers featuring CH32 series RISC-V MCUs with integrated USB; Wireless Connectivity including CH582 and CH579 BLE SoCs; and Interface Bridges for multi-protocol conversion. All products come with comprehensive driver support and development tools.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our product categories to explore QinHeng's complete portfolio, or contact our FAE team for product recommendations based on your interface requirements.

faqs.question: How do I select the right QinHeng USB converter for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right USB converter depends on your interface requirements. For basic UART conversion, CH340 is the most popular and cost-effective choice. For multi-protocol needs (UART/SPI/I2C/Parallel), CH341 offers flexibility. For high-speed or GPIO requirements, CH347 provides advanced features. Consider operating system compatibility, driver availability, and package options. Our FAE team can help evaluate your specific requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: CH340 for UART, CH341 for multi-protocol, CH347 for advanced features. Contact our FAE team for selection guidance.

faqs.question: What is the difference between CH340 and CH341?
faqs.answer: CH340 is a dedicated USB-to-UART converter with simple functionality and lowest cost. CH341 is a multi-function chip supporting USB-to-UART, SPI, I2C, and parallel interface. CH340 is ideal for simple serial communication while CH341 offers flexibility for applications requiring multiple interface types. Both chips use the same drivers and have similar USB performance characteristics.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use CH340 for simple UART applications. Use CH341 when you need SPI, I2C, or parallel interface support.

faqs.question: Does QinHeng provide drivers for Linux and macOS?
faqs.answer: Yes, QinHeng provides comprehensive driver support for all major operating systems. Windows drivers are WHQL certified. Linux drivers are built into kernel 2.6 and above (CH340/341 use usbserial driver). macOS drivers support 10.6 and above. Android support is available for devices with USB host mode. All drivers support both virtual COM port mode and direct API access.
faqs.decisionGuide: Drivers are available for all major OS platforms. Linux users typically don't need to install separate drivers. Contact our FAE team for driver integration support.

faqs.question: What development tools are available for CH32 RISC-V MCUs?
faqs.answer: QinHeng CH32 RISC-V microcontrollers are supported by open-source toolchains including GCC, OpenOCD, and PlatformIO. Official support includes WCH-Link debugger, SDK with HAL libraries, and application examples. Third-party IDEs like VS Code with PlatformIO extension provide comprehensive development environment. All tools are free and open-source.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use PlatformIO with VS Code for easiest setup. Use WCH-Link for debugging. Contact our FAE team for toolchain configuration assistance.

categories.name: USB-to-Serial Converters
categories.description: USB-to-serial converter chips for UART, SPI, I2C, and parallel interfaces
categories.longDescription: QinHeng's USB-to-serial converter portfolio provides reliable and cost-effective solutions for adding USB connectivity to embedded systems. The CH340 series is the most widely used USB-to-UART converter in the industry, known for its simplicity, stability, and broad OS compatibility. The CH341 adds multi-protocol support for applications requiring SPI, I2C, or parallel interfaces. These chips feature integrated USB transceivers, require minimal external components, and come with comprehensive driver support for Windows, Linux, and macOS. All converters support standard USB CDC class for plug-and-play operation and provide baud rates up to 2Mbps. As an authorized QinHeng distributor, we provide technical support, reference designs, and driver integration assistance.

series.name: CH340 Series
series.description: USB-to-UART converters with simple, reliable operation

series.name: CH341 Series
series.description: Multi-protocol USB converters with flexible interface options

selectionGuide.title: USB Converter Selection Guide
selectionGuide.content: How to select the right USB converter for your application

selectionGuideLink.text: How to select the right QinHeng USB converter?
selectionGuideLink.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting QinHeng USB converters based on interface requirements and OS compatibility

faqs.question: What is the maximum baud rate supported by CH340?
faqs.answer: CH340 supports baud rates from 50bps to 2Mbps. Common rates like 9600, 115200, 921600 are all supported. The actual maximum depends on the application and USB host capability. At 2Mbps, ensure your UART device can handle the speed and that cable quality is sufficient. The chip uses USB 2.0 Full Speed (12Mbps) interface, providing ample bandwidth for serial communication.
faqs.decisionGuide: CH340 supports all standard baud rates. For 2Mbps operation, verify your system can handle the speed. Contact our FAE team for high-speed application guidance.

faqs.question: How do I install CH340 drivers on Windows?
faqs.answer: Windows 10/11 include built-in CH340 drivers and typically install automatically. For older Windows versions or manual installation: 1) Download drivers from QinHeng website; 2) Run installer as administrator; 3) Connect CH340 device; 4) Windows will detect and install driver; 5) Verify in Device Manager under Ports (COM & LPT). If driver doesn't install automatically, manually point to driver folder. WHQL certified drivers ensure compatibility.
faqs.decisionGuide: Windows 10/11 users typically don't need manual installation. For older Windows or issues, download drivers from our website. Contact our FAE team for driver troubleshooting.

faqs.question: Can CH340 operate at 3.3V IO voltage?
faqs.answer: Yes, CH340 supports both 5V and 3.3V IO voltage levels. The VCC pin determines IO voltage - connect to 5V for 5V IO, or 3.3V for 3.3V IO. The USB interface remains at USB standard levels regardless of IO voltage. This flexibility allows direct connection to 3.3V microcontrollers without level shifting. Ensure your PCB design matches the intended IO voltage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Connect VCC to desired IO voltage (5V or 3.3V). No external level shifter needed for 3.3V systems. Contact our FAE team for mixed-voltage system design.

faqs.question: What is the difference between CH340G and CH340C?
faqs.answer: CH340G and CH340C are pin-compatible variants with different clock sources. CH340G requires external 12MHz crystal oscillator. CH340C has built-in clock oscillator, eliminating external crystal and reducing BOM cost. CH340C is recommended for new designs due to simpler design and lower cost. Both offer identical USB and UART functionality. CH340E is a smaller package variant with fewer handshake signals.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use CH340C for new designs (no crystal needed). Use CH340G if you need external clock source. Contact our FAE team for variant selection.

faqs.question: How do I use CH341 in SPI or I2C mode?
faqs.answer: CH341 operates in different modes selected by configuration pins or EEPROM. For SPI mode: configure MODE0/MODE1 pins for SPI, connect to SPI device using SCK, MOSI, MISO pins. For I2C mode: configure for I2C, use SDA and SCL pins. The CH341 DLL/API provides functions for SPI/I2C transactions. Windows drivers include DLL interface. Linux uses i2c-dev or spidev interface. Application examples are provided in QinHeng SDK.
faqs.decisionGuide: Configure CH341 mode pins for desired interface. Use provided DLL/API for communication. Contact our FAE team for SPI/I2C application examples.

products.name: USB to UART Converter
products.shortDescription: Popular USB-to-UART converter with built-in oscillator, 5V/3.3V IO, up to 2Mbps baud rate
descriptionParagraphs: CH340C is the most widely used USB-to-UART converter chip, featuring integrated clock oscillator that eliminates external crystal.
With support for baud rates up to 2Mbps and both 5V/3.3V IO levels, this chip is ideal for adding USB connectivity to microcontrollers and embedded systems.
The chip requires minimal external components and comes with comprehensive driver support for Windows, Linux, and macOS.

specifications.Handshake: RTS, CTS, DTR, DSR, DCD, RI

features: Built-in clock oscillator - no crystal needed

faeReview.author: David Liu
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Interface Solutions
faeReview.content: CH340C is my go-to recommendation for USB-to-UART applications. The built-in oscillator is a game-changer - it eliminates the external crystal, reducing BOM cost and PCB space. I've used this chip in dozens of projects from industrial controllers to consumer electronics. The driver support is excellent - plug-and-play on modern Windows and built-in Linux support. The 2Mbps baud rate handles most high-speed serial needs. One tip: always include the 100nF decoupling capacitor close to the chip, and ensure your PCB layout keeps the USB traces short and matched.
faeReview.highlight: Built-in oscillator eliminates external crystal, reducing BOM cost

alternativeParts.comparison: CH340C=><CP2102: Package QFN vs SOP (QinHeng easier to solder), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.reason: Alternative with similar functionality but higher cost
alternativeParts.useCase: Suitable for applications requiring Silicon Labs ecosystem

alternativeParts.reason: Higher performance alternative with advanced features

faqs.question: What is the maximum baud rate of CH340C?
faqs.answer: CH340C supports baud rates from 50bps up to 2Mbps. The actual maximum depends on your application requirements and USB host capability. At 2Mbps, ensure your UART device and cable quality can support the speed. The chip uses USB 2.0 Full Speed interface with 12Mbps bandwidth, providing reliable communication at all supported baud rates.
faqs.decisionGuide: 2Mbps supports most high-speed serial applications. For higher speeds, consider USB HS solutions. Contact our FAE team for baud rate selection guidance.

faqs.question: Does CH340C require an external crystal?
faqs.answer: No, CH340C has a built-in clock oscillator and does not require an external crystal. This reduces BOM cost and PCB space compared to CH340G which requires external 12MHz crystal. The internal oscillator provides stable clock for USB and UART operation across the full temperature range. This is the main advantage of CH340C over CH340G.
faqs.decisionGuide: CH340C is recommended for new designs. Use CH340G only if you need external clock source for synchronization. Contact our FAE team for variant selection.

faqs.question: How do I configure CH340C for 3.3V operation?
faqs.answer: CH340C IO voltage is determined by the VCC pin voltage. Connect VCC to 3.3V for 3.3V IO operation, or 5V for 5V IO operation. The USB interface operates at standard USB levels regardless of VCC. No external level shifting is needed when connecting to 3.3V microcontrollers. Ensure all IO pins stay within 0V to VCC range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Connect VCC to your system voltage (3.3V or 5V). IO levels will match VCC. Contact our FAE team for mixed-voltage system design.

faqs.question: What drivers are needed for CH340C?
faqs.answer: Windows 10/11 include built-in CH340 drivers and typically install automatically. For older Windows versions, download drivers from QinHeng website. Linux kernel 2.6+ includes built-in driver (usbserial). macOS drivers are available for download. All drivers support virtual COM port mode. Android support available for USB host mode devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: Modern OS typically auto-install drivers. Download from website if needed. Contact our FAE team for driver integration support.

faqs.question: What is the typical application circuit for CH340C?
faqs.answer: Basic CH340C circuit requires: 1) VCC connected to 3.3V or 5V; 2) GND connection; 3) 100nF decoupling capacitor on VCC; 4) USB D+ and D- connected to USB connector; 5) TXD and RXD to UART device; 6) Optional handshake signals (RTS/CTS). No external crystal needed. Keep USB traces short and matched. Reference schematic available in datasheet.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow reference schematic in datasheet. Keep decoupling capacitor close to chip. Contact our FAE team for PCB layout review.

products.name: USB to UART Converter with External Crystal
products.shortDescription: USB-to-UART converter requiring external 12MHz crystal, pin-compatible with CH340C for legacy designs
descriptionParagraphs: CH340G is a USB-to-UART converter similar to CH340C but requires an external 12MHz crystal oscillator.
This variant is pin-compatible with CH340C and can be used in legacy designs or applications requiring external clock synchronization.
The chip supports baud rates up to 2Mbps and both 5V/3.3V IO levels with comprehensive driver support.

specifications.Handshake: RTS, CTS, DTR, DSR, DCD, RI

faeReview.author: David Liu
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Interface Solutions
faeReview.content: CH340G is the predecessor to CH340C and requires an external crystal. I recommend CH340C for all new designs, but CH340G is useful for maintaining compatibility with existing PCB layouts or when external clock synchronization is required. The performance and features are identical to CH340C - only the clock source differs. If you're designing a new product, use CH340C to save the crystal cost and PCB space.

products.name: Multi-Protocol USB Converter
products.shortDescription: USB-to-UART/SPI/I2C/Parallel converter with flexible interface options and EEPROM configuration
descriptionParagraphs: CH341A is a versatile multi-protocol USB converter supporting UART, SPI, I2C, and parallel interfaces.
With EEPROM configuration and hardware flow control, this chip provides flexibility for complex embedded applications.
The chip is ideal for applications requiring multiple interface types or upgrading legacy parallel devices to USB.

faeReview.author: Michael Zhang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Interface Solutions
faeReview.content: CH341A is incredibly versatile for applications needing multiple interface types. I've used it in universal programmers that need to support SPI Flash, I2C EEPROM, and UART devices. The EEPROM configuration allows the chip to remember settings between power cycles. One consideration: the SPI speed is limited to 5MHz, so it's not suitable for high-speed Flash programming. For those applications, use CH347 instead. The parallel interface mode is useful for upgrading legacy LPT devices to USB.
faeReview.highlight: Multi-protocol flexibility in a single chip

products.name: High-Speed USB to Multi-Protocol Bridge
products.shortDescription: High-speed USB bridge supporting SPI up to 30MHz, I2C, UART, and GPIO for development tools
descriptionParagraphs: CH347 is a high-performance USB bridge chip optimized for development tools and high-speed programming applications.
With SPI speeds up to 30MHz and UART up to 3Mbps, this chip significantly outperforms CH341 for speed-critical applications.
The chip supports simultaneous operation of multiple interfaces and provides GPIO for additional control.

faeReview.author: Kevin Wang
faeReview.title: FAE - Development Tools
faeReview.content: CH347 is my top recommendation for development tools requiring high-speed SPI. The 30MHz SPI speed is 6x faster than CH341, making it ideal for programming large Flash memories. I use it in a universal programmer that handles everything from SPI Flash to I2C EEPROMs. The simultaneous operation of multiple interfaces is very useful - you can program a device while monitoring UART debug output. The GPIO pins add flexibility for controlling target power or reset. Highly recommended for tool developers.

categories.name: USB Microcontrollers
categories.description: RISC-V microcontrollers with integrated USB peripherals for embedded applications
categories.longDescription: QinHeng's CH32 series microcontrollers feature QingKe RISC-V cores with integrated USB peripherals, offering a cost-effective alternative to ARM-based solutions. These MCUs provide USB 2.0 Full Speed device and host capability, rich analog and digital peripherals, and competitive pricing. The CH32V307 features a high-performance 144MHz RISC-V4F core with FPU, while the CH32X035 offers an ultra-low-cost option for basic USB applications. All devices are supported by open-source toolchains including GCC and PlatformIO, eliminating licensing costs. The integrated USB bootloader simplifies firmware updates without external programmers.

series.name: CH32V3 Series
series.description: High-performance RISC-V4F MCUs with FPU up to 144MHz

series.name: CH32X0 Series
series.description: Cost-effective RISC-V3A MCUs for USB applications

selectionGuide.title: MCU Selection Guide
selectionGuide.content: How to select the right QinHeng MCU for your application

selectionGuideLink.text: How to select the right QinHeng RISC-V MCU?
selectionGuideLink.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting QinHeng RISC-V microcontrollers

faqs.question: What development tools support CH32 MCUs?
faqs.answer: CH32 microcontrollers are supported by open-source toolchains including GCC, OpenOCD, and PlatformIO. Official WCH-Link debugger provides programming and debugging capability. MounRiver Studio is the official IDE based on Eclipse. VS Code with PlatformIO extension provides a modern development environment. All tools are free and open-source, with no licensing fees required.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use PlatformIO with VS Code for easiest setup. Use WCH-Link for debugging. Contact our FAE team for toolchain configuration.

products.name: RISC-V4F MCU with USB
products.shortDescription: High-performance 144MHz RISC-V4F MCU with FPU, USB 2.0, up to 256KB Flash Ideal for industrial and consumer applications with excellent performance and reliability.
descriptionParagraphs: CH32V307 is a high-performance RISC-V microcontroller featuring the QingKe RISC-V4F core running at up to 144MHz with hardware FPU.
With USB 2.0 High Speed and Full Speed support, rich analog peripherals including 2x ADCs and DAC, this MCU is ideal for advanced embedded applications.
The chip includes up to 256KB Flash and 64KB RAM, multiple timers, and extensive communication interfaces including CAN, SDIO, and Ethernet.
features: USB 2.0 High Speed (480Mbps) and Full Speed

faeReview.author: Michael Zhang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - MCU Applications
faeReview.content: CH32V307 is an impressive RISC-V MCU that offers ARM Cortex-M4 class performance at a fraction of the cost. The 144MHz RISC-V4F core with FPU handles DSP algorithms efficiently. I've successfully used this MCU in motor control applications where the FPU significantly improved control loop performance. The USB 2.0 High Speed capability is rare in this price range. The open-source toolchain support means no licensing costs, making it ideal for cost-sensitive projects. One consideration: the peripheral library is not as mature as STM32 HAL, but it's improving rapidly.
faeReview.highlight: ARM-class performance with RISC-V open architecture at low cost

alternativeParts.comparison: CH32V307=><STM32F407: Frequency 168MHz > 144MHz (STM faster), suitable for direct replacement
alternativeParts.reason: Higher performance alternative with more memory

faqs.question: What is the maximum clock speed of CH32V307?
faqs.answer: CH32V307 supports up to 144MHz system clock. The QingKe RISC-V4F core includes hardware FPU that operates at full clock speed. The chip includes PLL for clock multiplication from external crystal or internal oscillator. Power consumption scales with clock frequency, allowing power/performance trade-off for battery applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: 144MHz provides ARM Cortex-M4 class performance. Lower frequency for power saving. Contact our FAE team for clock configuration.

products.name: RISC-V3A MCU with USB
products.shortDescription: Cost-effective 72MHz RISC-V3A MCU with USB 2.0, up to 64KB Flash for basic embedded applications
descriptionParagraphs: CH32V203 is a cost-effective RISC-V microcontroller featuring the QingKe RISC-V3A core running at up to 72MHz.
With USB 2.0 Full Speed device support, basic analog peripherals, and compact packages, this MCU is ideal for cost-sensitive USB applications.
The chip includes up to 64KB Flash and 20KB RAM, making it suitable for USB HID, CDC, and simple control applications.

faeReview.author: Michael Zhang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - MCU Applications
faeReview.content: CH32V203 is the perfect entry point into RISC-V for cost-sensitive applications. At a fraction of the cost of ARM alternatives, it provides sufficient performance for basic USB applications. I've used this MCU in USB HID devices like custom keyboards and mice. The 72MHz core is plenty for these applications, and the USB bootloader makes firmware updates easy. The compact QFN32 package is great for space-constrained designs. For applications requiring more memory or peripherals, step up to CH32V307.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-low-cost RISC-V for basic USB applications

products.name: Low-Cost RISC-V MCU
products.shortDescription: Ultra-low-cost 48MHz RISC-V3A MCU with USB, optimized for simple control and HID applications
descriptionParagraphs: CH32X035 is an ultra-low-cost RISC-V microcontroller designed for the most cost-sensitive applications.
With a 48MHz RISC-V3A core and USB 2.0 Full Speed device support, this MCU is ideal for simple USB HID and control applications.
The chip includes up to 32KB Flash and 10KB RAM in very compact packages, making it perfect for space and cost-constrained designs.

faeReview.author: Sarah Chen
faeReview.title: FAE - MCU Applications
faeReview.content: CH32X035 is the most cost-effective RISC-V MCU I've worked with. It's perfect for simple USB HID applications where cost is the primary concern. I've used it in custom USB input devices and simple control applications. The 48MHz core is sufficient for these tasks, and the USB bootloader simplifies production programming. The TSSOP20 and SOP16 packages are easy to hand-solder for prototypes. Don't expect high performance - this is a basic MCU for basic tasks - but the price is unbeatable.
faeReview.highlight: Lowest-cost RISC-V MCU for simple applications

products.name: RISC-V MCU with BLE
products.shortDescription: RISC-V4F MCU with integrated BLE 5.3, 144MHz core, for wireless IoT applications
descriptionParagraphs: CH32V208 combines a high-performance 144MHz RISC-V4F core with integrated BLE 5.3 wireless connectivity.
With up to 256KB Flash, 64KB RAM, and rich peripherals, this MCU is ideal for wireless IoT applications requiring both processing power and connectivity.
The chip supports BLE long-range mode, 2Mbps high-speed, and multiple sleep modes for battery-powered applications.

faeReview.author: Michael Zhang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - MCU Applications
faeReview.content: CH32V208 is an impressive wireless MCU that combines RISC-V performance with BLE 5.3 connectivity. The 144MHz core with FPU handles complex algorithms while the integrated BLE eliminates the need for a separate wireless chip. I've used this in IoT sensor nodes where the long-range BLE mode provides excellent coverage. The multiple sleep modes enable multi-year battery life with coin cells. The USB interface is handy for firmware updates and debugging. Highly recommended for wireless IoT applications requiring processing power.
faeReview.highlight: RISC-V + BLE 5.3 integration for wireless IoT

categories.name: Wireless Connectivity
categories.description: Bluetooth Low Energy SoCs for IoT and wireless applications
categories.longDescription: QinHeng's wireless connectivity portfolio includes Bluetooth Low Energy SoCs combining RISC-V cores with 2.4GHz transceivers. The CH582 features BLE 5.3 with long-range and high-speed modes, while the CH579 offers a cost-effective BLE 4.2 solution with integrated USB. These chips provide complete wireless connectivity for IoT devices, beacons, and wireless sensors with minimal external components.

selectionGuide.title: Wireless SoC Selection Guide
selectionGuide.content: How to select the right wireless chip

selectionGuideLink.text: How to select the right BLE SoC?
selectionGuideLink.description: Guide for selecting QinHeng wireless connectivity chips

faqs.question: What Bluetooth versions are supported?
faqs.answer: CH582 supports Bluetooth 5.3 with features like long-range (Coded PHY), 2Mbps high speed, and extended advertising. CH579 supports Bluetooth 4.2. Both support BLE peripheral and central modes. The BLE stack runs on the integrated RISC-V core with minimal CPU overhead.
faqs.decisionGuide: CH582 for latest BLE 5.3 features. CH579 for basic BLE 4.2 applications. Contact our FAE team for BLE protocol guidance.

products.name: BLE 5.3 SoC
products.shortDescription: Bluetooth 5.3 SoC with RISC-V core, long-range, 2Mbps support Ideal for industrial and consumer applications with excellent performance and reliability.
descriptionParagraphs: CH582 is a Bluetooth 5.3 SoC featuring integrated RISC-V core and 2.4GHz transceiver.
With support for long-range mode, 2Mbps high-speed, and extended advertising, this chip is ideal for modern IoT applications.
The chip includes 448KB Flash, 32KB RAM, and rich peripherals for sensor interfacing.

faeReview.author: Sarah Chen
faeReview.title: FAE - Wireless Applications
faeReview.content: CH582 offers excellent value for BLE applications. The BLE 5.3 long-range mode provides 2-4x range improvement over BLE 4.2, making it ideal for industrial IoT. The integrated RISC-V core eliminates need for separate host MCU. Power consumption is competitive with Nordic and Silicon Labs alternatives. The SDK includes comprehensive BLE stack and examples. One limitation: the RISC-V core is not as fast as Cortex-M4, but sufficient for most BLE applications.
faeReview.highlight: BLE 5.3 long-range at competitive price point

alternativeParts.reason: Higher performance alternative with more memory
alternativeParts.useCase: Best for complex applications requiring 1MB Flash

faqs.question: What is the range of CH582 in long-range mode?
faqs.answer: In BLE 5.3 long-range mode (Coded PHY, 125kbps or 500kbps), CH582 can achieve 2-4x range compared to standard 1Mbps mode. Actual range depends on environment, antenna design, and obstacles. Typical indoor range is 50-100m, outdoor line-of-sight can exceed 300m. Use with appropriate antenna for best performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use long-range mode for maximum distance. Standard mode for higher data rates. Contact our FAE team for antenna design.

products.name: BLE 4.2 SoC with USB
products.shortDescription: Cost-effective BLE 4.2 SoC with RISC-V core, USB, and low power for basic wireless applications
descriptionParagraphs: CH579 is a cost-effective Bluetooth Low Energy SoC featuring integrated RISC-V core and USB 2.0 interface.
With BLE 4.2 support, 448KB Flash, and multiple low-power modes, this chip is ideal for basic wireless IoT applications.
The integrated USB provides convenient firmware updates and debugging capabilities.

faeReview.author: Sarah Chen
faeReview.title: FAE - Wireless Applications
faeReview.content: CH579 is the cost-effective alternative to CH582 for applications that don't need BLE 5.3 features. It provides reliable BLE 4.2 connectivity at a lower price point. I've used this in simple beacon applications and basic wireless sensors where the older BLE standard is sufficient. The integrated USB is convenient for firmware updates. Battery life is excellent with proper power management. For new designs requiring long-range or 2Mbps modes, upgrade to CH582, but CH579 remains a solid choice for cost-sensitive applications.
faeReview.highlight: Cost-effective BLE 4.2 for basic wireless applications

products.name: BLE 5.3 SoC with Enhanced Features
products.shortDescription: Advanced BLE 5.3 SoC with extended temperature range, enhanced security for industrial IoT
descriptionParagraphs: CH592 is an enhanced BLE 5.3 SoC designed for industrial and commercial applications requiring extended temperature range and robust operation.
With advanced security features, extended temperature range, and high RF performance, this chip is ideal for demanding IoT deployments.
The chip includes 512KB Flash, 64KB RAM, and supports BLE 5.3 long-range and high-speed modes.

faeReview.author: Kevin Liu
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Wireless Applications
faeReview.content: CH592 is designed for industrial applications where reliability and extended temperature range are critical. The -40°C to +105°C range handles harsh environments that consumer-grade chips cannot. The enhanced security features including AES-256 and ECC are essential for commercial IoT deployments. I've used this in industrial sensor networks where the +8dBm TX power provides excellent range. The improved sensitivity (-97dBm) ensures reliable connections in challenging RF environments. For industrial IoT, this is my top recommendation.
faeReview.highlight: Industrial-grade BLE 5.3 with extended temperature range

products.name: Ultra-Low-Power BLE SoC
products.shortDescription: Ultra-low-power BLE 5.1 SoC optimized for battery-powered sensors with multi-year operation
descriptionParagraphs: CH591 is an ultra-low-power BLE SoC designed for battery-powered applications requiring multi-year operation on coin cells.
With sub-microamp sleep currents and efficient BLE 5.1 operation, this chip enables IoT sensors that run for years on small batteries.
The chip includes 256KB Flash, 32KB RAM, and optimized power management for beacon and sensor applications.

faeReview.author: Sarah Chen
faeReview.title: FAE - Wireless Applications
faeReview.content: CH591 is specifically designed for ultra-long battery life applications. The 0.5uA sleep current is exceptional - I've designed beacons that run 5+ years on CR2032 cells using this chip. The BLE 5.1 long-range mode extends coverage while maintaining low power. The WLCSP package is tiny - perfect for wearables and compact sensors. For battery-powered IoT, this is the chip I recommend first. Just be aware that the smaller Flash (256KB) limits application complexity compared to CH582.
faeReview.highlight: Sub-microamp sleep current for multi-year battery life

categories.name: Interface Bridges
categories.description: Multi-protocol USB bridge chips for flexible connectivity
categories.longDescription: QinHeng's interface bridge chips provide flexible USB-to-serial conversion for multiple protocols. The CH347 supports SPI, I2C, UART, and GPIO through a single USB interface, ideal for development tools and multi-protocol applications. These chips eliminate the need for multiple converters and provide a unified interface for embedded development.

selectionGuideLink.description: Guide for selecting QinHeng interface bridges

faqs.question: What protocols does CH347 support?
faqs.answer: CH347 supports SPI (up to 30MHz), I2C (up to 1MHz), UART (up to 3Mbps), and GPIO through USB 2.0 interface. Multiple interfaces can be used simultaneously. The chip appears as multiple virtual COM ports or can be accessed through DLL API for direct control.
faqs.decisionGuide: CH347 for multi-protocol needs. Use dedicated chips for single-protocol cost optimization. Contact our FAE team for protocol configuration.

products.name: USB Multi-Protocol Bridge
products.shortDescription: USB to SPI/I2C/UART/GPIO bridge with high-speed operation Ideal for industrial and consumer applications with excellent performance and reliability.
descriptionParagraphs: CH347 is a flexible USB bridge chip supporting multiple serial protocols through a single USB interface.
With SPI up to 30MHz, I2C up to 1MHz, and UART up to 3Mbps, this chip is ideal for development tools and test equipment.
The chip provides GPIO pins for additional control and status monitoring.

faeReview.author: Kevin Wang
faeReview.title: FAE - Development Tools
faeReview.content: CH347 is perfect for development tools requiring multiple protocols. I use it in a universal programmer that supports SPI Flash, I2C EEPROM, and UART devices. The 30MHz SPI speed handles high-speed Flash programming. The unified USB interface simplifies the design compared to using multiple converter chips. The DLL API provides fine control over timing for picky devices. Highly recommended for tool developers.

faqs.question: Can CH347 operate all interfaces simultaneously?
faqs.answer: Yes, CH347 can operate SPI, I2C, and UART interfaces simultaneously through the USB interface. The chip multiplexes data over USB. Each interface has independent configuration and buffering. Total USB bandwidth is shared among active interfaces. For high-throughput applications, consider the aggregate data rate.
faqs.decisionGuide: All interfaces can be active simultaneously. Monitor total bandwidth for high-throughput applications. Contact our FAE team for multi-interface configuration.

products.name: USB to Parallel/SPI/I2C Bridge
products.shortDescription: Flexible USB bridge supporting parallel port, SPI, and I2C for legacy device upgrades
descriptionParagraphs: CH341 is a versatile USB bridge chip designed for upgrading legacy parallel port devices to USB.
With support for parallel port, SPI, and I2C interfaces, this chip provides a migration path for older equipment.
The chip is widely used in programmers, EPROM emulators, and industrial control interfaces.

faeReview.author: David Liu
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Interface Solutions
faeReview.content: CH341 is the go-to solution for upgrading legacy parallel port devices. I've helped dozens of customers migrate old LPT-based equipment to USB using this chip. The EPP mode support is crucial for many industrial applications. While the SPI speed is limited to 5MHz, it's sufficient for configuration Flash and small EEPROMs. The built-in oscillator keeps the BOM simple. For new designs requiring higher SPI speeds, use CH347 instead. But for parallel port replacement, CH341 is unmatched.

products.name: USB to Serial Port Expander
products.shortDescription: USB to dual UART bridge with independent baud rates for multi-port applications
descriptionParagraphs: CH9326 is a USB-to-dual-UART bridge chip providing two independent serial ports from a single USB connection.
With independent baud rate configuration and hardware flow control, this chip is ideal for applications requiring multiple serial interfaces.
The chip supports baud rates up to 2Mbps on both ports simultaneously.

faeReview.author: Michael Zhang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Interface Solutions
faeReview.content: CH9326 is perfect for applications needing dual serial ports. I've used it in industrial gateways that bridge between USB and multiple RS-485 networks. The independent baud rates are crucial when connecting to different devices. The 256-byte FIFOs prevent data loss at high baud rates. Both ports appear as separate COM ports in Windows, making software integration easy. The chip is reliable and the drivers are stable. Highly recommended for multi-port applications.
faeReview.highlight: Dual UART ports from single USB connection

products.name: USB to Quad UART Bridge
products.shortDescription: USB to 4-channel UART bridge for high-density serial applications and multi-device connectivity
descriptionParagraphs: CH9344 is a high-density USB-to-quad-UART bridge chip providing four independent serial ports from a single USB interface.
With support for baud rates up to 2Mbps on all four ports, this chip is ideal for high-density serial applications.
The chip includes large FIFOs and hardware flow control for reliable high-speed operation.

faeReview.author: Kevin Wang
faeReview.title: FAE - Interface Solutions
faeReview.content: CH9344 is the solution when you need maximum serial port density. I've designed it into test systems that need to communicate with multiple devices simultaneously. The 512-byte FIFOs are larger than most competitors, preventing overflow during bursts. All four ports work independently at full speed without interfering with each other. The QFN40 package is compact for the functionality provided. Driver installation creates four separate COM ports automatically. Excellent for industrial and test applications.
faeReview.highlight: Four UART ports with large FIFOs for high-density applications
